description: Convert unsigned integer values into IPv4 addresses for debugging, scripting, databases, logging, and network calculations. Useful when working with numeric IP storage formats.

faq.q: What input does Integer to IP accept?
faq.a: It accepts unsigned 32-bit integer values such as 3232235777 and can also handle hex input like 0xC0A80101.

faq.q: What does the output show?
faq.a: It returns the corresponding dotted IPv4 address.

name: MAC Address Lookup
intro: Inspect MAC addresses and check common vendor prefixes.
description: Normalize MAC addresses, detect address type flags, and check for common built-in OUI vendor prefix matches. Useful for inventory work, network troubleshooting, and device identification.

faq.q: What does MAC Address Lookup show?
faq.a: It shows the normalized MAC address, address type flags, OUI prefix, and a likely vendor match from a built-in common prefix list when available.

faq.q: Can it detect multicast and locally administered addresses?
faq.a: Yes. It checks broadcast, multicast, unicast, and locally administered bits.

name: MAC Address Generator
intro: Generate random MAC addresses with optional format settings.
description: Generate random MAC addresses with configurable count, separator style, prefix, uppercase output, and address type options. Useful for labs, testing, scripts, and sample network data.

faq.q: Can I generate multiple MAC addresses at once?
faq.a: Yes. You can provide a count value and generate multiple MAC addresses in one run.

faq.q: Can I control the format?
faq.a: Yes. You can choose separators, uppercase output, optional prefix, and address type options.

name: SHA1 Hash Generator
intro: Generate SHA-1 hashes from text in the browser.
description: Generate SHA-1 hashes from plain text for quick checks, comparisons, legacy workflows, and debugging. Useful when you need a fast SHA-1 output without external tools.

faq.q: What does SHA1 Hash Generator do?
faq.a: It hashes your input text and returns the SHA-1 digest in hexadecimal format.

faq.q: When is it useful?
faq.a: It is useful for quick comparisons, scripts, debugging, and legacy systems that still reference SHA-1.

name: SHA512 Hash Generator
intro: Generate SHA-512 hashes from text quickly in the browser.
description: Generate SHA-512 hashes from plain text for integrity checks, scripts, comparisons, and debugging. Useful when you need a strong hash output without external tools.

faq.q: What output format does it return?
faq.a: It returns the SHA-512 digest as a hexadecimal string.

faq.q: Who is this useful for?
faq.a: It is useful for developers, admins, students, and anyone who needs fast hash output for testing or comparison.

name: Bcrypt Hash Generator
intro: Generate bcrypt hashes with configurable cost.
description: Generate bcrypt hashes from text with an adjustable cost factor. Useful for password hashing demos, development, testing, and understanding bcrypt output structure.

faq.q: Can I control the cost factor?
faq.a: Yes. You can provide a cost value, and the tool will generate the bcrypt hash using that work factor.

faq.q: What kind of input can I use?
faq.a: You can paste plain text directly or use JSON or key=value input with text and cost.

name: HMAC Generator
intro: Generate HMAC digests using a secret key and message.
description: Generate HMAC values using a secret key, message, and selectable hash algorithm such as SHA-1, SHA-256, or SHA-512. Useful for APIs, signatures, webhooks, and debugging signed requests.

faq.q: What algorithms does HMAC Generator support?
faq.a: It supports common HMAC algorithms such as SHA-1, SHA-256, and SHA-512.

faq.q: What input formats are supported?
faq.a: You can use JSON, key=value lines, or simple text input with message and key fields.

name: Hex Encoder
intro: Encode text into hexadecimal output in the browser.
description: Convert plain text into hexadecimal encoding for debugging, payload inspection, scripting, and quick transformations. Useful for developers, networking labs, and data conversion tasks.

faq.q: What does Hex Encoder convert?
faq.a: It converts your input text into hexadecimal byte output using UTF-8 encoding.

faq.q: Can I use it for copied text and payloads?
faq.a: Yes. It works well for plain text, snippets, payloads, and quick byte-level inspection.

name: Hex Decoder
intro: Decode hexadecimal text back into readable output.
description: Convert hexadecimal input back into plain text using UTF-8 decoding. Useful for debugging payloads, inspecting encoded strings, reversing hex output, and quick developer tasks.

faq.q: What does Hex Decoder do?
faq.a: It converts hexadecimal byte input back into readable text using UTF-8 decoding.

faq.q: Can it decode spaced hex input?
faq.a: Yes. It can handle common hex input with spaces or line breaks.

name: Base16 Encode
intro: Encode text into Base16 output in uppercase hexadecimal form.
description: Convert plain text into Base16 encoding using uppercase hexadecimal output. Useful for developer tools, payload inspection, debugging, and workflows where Base16 naming matches search intent.

faq.q: Is Base16 the same as hexadecimal?
faq.a: Yes. Base16 is standard hexadecimal encoding, usually written with characters 0-9 and A-F.

faq.q: What output format does this tool use?
faq.a: It returns uppercase Base16 output without separators.

name: Base16 Decode
intro: Decode Base16 text back into readable UTF-8 output.
description: Convert Base16 hexadecimal input back into plain text. Useful for debugging, payload inspection, quick decoding tasks, and pages targeting Base16 search intent.

faq.q: Can Base16 Decode handle lowercase input?
faq.a: Yes. It accepts both uppercase and lowercase hexadecimal input.

faq.q: What does the output return?
faq.a: It returns decoded UTF-8 text when the Base16 input is valid.

name: Base85 Encode
intro: Encode text into Base85 output for compact text-safe representation.
description: Convert plain text into Base85 output using ASCII85-compatible encoding. Useful for compact text encoding, debugging, payload work, and developer workflows.

faq.q: What Base85 format does this tool use?
faq.a: It uses ASCII85-compatible Base85 output without wrapper markers by default.

faq.q: Why use Base85 instead of Base64?
faq.a: Base85 can be more compact than Base64 for some workflows while still staying text-friendly.

name: Base85 Decode
intro: Decode Base85 text back into readable UTF-8 output.
description: Convert Base85 ASCII85-compatible input back into plain text. Useful for debugging compact encoded payloads, reversing encoded strings, and working with developer data formats.

faq.q: Can it decode ASCII85 input with markers?
faq.a: Yes. It can handle common ASCII85-style input and ignores optional wrapper markers like <~ and ~>.

faq.q: Does it support compressed z blocks?
faq.a: Yes. It supports the common ASCII85 z shorthand for four zero bytes.

name: URL-safe Base64 Decoder
intro: Decode URL-safe Base64 strings back into readable text.
description: Decode URL-safe Base64 input by handling - and _ characters, missing padding, and UTF-8 text output. Useful for tokens, URLs, payloads, and developer debugging.

faq.q: What does URL-safe Base64 Decoder handle?
faq.a: It handles Base64URL input with - and _ characters and automatically restores missing padding when needed.

faq.q: What is this useful for?
faq.a: It is useful for decoding tokens, URL parameters, payload fragments, and Base64URL-encoded text.

name: Regex Generator
intro: Generate common regex patterns from simple input.
description: Generate regex patterns for common cases such as email, URL, IPv4, slug, UUID, username, phone, date, and more. Useful for developers, form validation, and quick pattern building.

faq.q: What kinds of patterns can Regex Generator create?
faq.a: It can generate common patterns such as email, URL, IPv4, slug, UUID, username, phone, date, and numeric formats.

faq.q: Can I control anchors and flags?
faq.a: Yes. You can provide simple options such as anchored and flags using JSON or key=value lines.

name: Regex Escape
intro: Escape special characters for safe use inside regular expressions.
description: Escape regex special characters such as dots, plus signs, parentheses, brackets, and question marks so text can be used literally inside a regular expression pattern.

faq.q: What does Regex Escape do?
faq.a: It escapes regex metacharacters so your input text can be used as a literal string inside a regular expression.

faq.q: When should I use it?
faq.a: Use it when you need to match exact text that contains regex symbols like ., +, ?, (, ), [, or ].

name: Regex Match Extractor
intro: Extract regex matches and groups from text input.
description: Extract all regex matches, match positions, capturing groups, and named groups from text. Useful for debugging patterns, parsing data, and testing regular expressions quickly.

faq.q: What does Regex Match Extractor return?
faq.a: It returns all matches, their index positions, and any capturing groups or named groups.

faq.q: How do I provide the pattern and text?
faq.a: You can use JSON input or key=value lines with pattern, flags, and text.

name: Lorem Ipsum Generator
intro: Generate lorem ipsum words, sentences, or paragraphs.
description: Generate predictable lorem ipsum placeholder text as words, sentences, or paragraphs. Useful for mockups, UI placeholders, templates, content demos, and quick text filling.

faq.q: What kinds of output can Lorem Ipsum Generator create?
faq.a: It can generate words, sentences, or paragraphs depending on the selected input settings.

faq.q: Can I control the amount?
faq.a: Yes. You can set the count and unit using JSON or key=value lines.

name: API Request Body Formatter
intro: Format API request bodies into cleaner readable output.
description: Format API request bodies from JSON, key=value lines, or query-string style input into a cleaner readable structure. Useful for API testing, debugging requests, documentation, and payload cleanup.

faq.q: What input formats does API Request Body Formatter support?
faq.a: It supports JSON, key=value lines, and URL-encoded query-string style input.

faq.q: What does the output look like?
faq.a: It returns a neatly formatted JSON-style structure for easier reading and debugging.

name: JSON Diff Checker
intro: Compare two JSON values and show the differences clearly.
description: Compare two JSON objects or arrays and show added, removed, changed, and type-changed values by path. Useful for APIs, configs, test fixtures, payload debugging, and version comparison.

faq.q: How do I provide the two JSON values?
faq.a: You can use JSON input with left and right fields or separate the two blocks with a line containing ---.

faq.q: What kinds of differences does it show?
faq.a: It shows added, removed, changed, and type-changed values together with their JSON paths.

name: Text Diff Checker
intro: Compare two text blocks and show line-by-line differences.
description: Compare two text blocks and show which lines are equal, added, removed, or changed. Useful for notes, configs, code snippets, copied content, and quick text comparison.

faq.q: How do I provide the two text blocks?
faq.a: You can use JSON with left and right fields or separate the two text blocks with a line containing ---.

faq.q: What does the output show?
faq.a: It shows a summary plus line-by-line diff results with line numbers and change types.

name: Reverse DNS Lookup
intro: Look up the PTR hostname for an IPv4 address.
description: Use this Reverse DNS Lookup tool to find the PTR hostname linked to an IPv4 address. It is useful for troubleshooting, server checks, and DNS verification.

faq.q: What does Reverse DNS Lookup do?
faq.a: It converts an IP address into a PTR lookup query and tries to find the hostname assigned to that IP.

faq.q: Can I use it for any IP address?
faq.a: Yes, for public IPv4 addresses. If no PTR record exists, the tool will show no result.

name: WHOIS Lookup
intro: Check registration and ownership details for a domain or IP.
description: Use this WHOIS Lookup tool to view registration details for a domain or IP address. It is useful for domain research, ownership checks, and technical investigation.

faq.q: What can WHOIS Lookup show?
faq.a: It can show registrar data, registration dates, nameservers, status values, and other public registration details.

faq.q: Can I look up both domains and IP addresses?
faq.a: Yes. This tool supports both domain and IPv4 input.

name: MX Lookup
intro: Find MX records for a domain.
description: Use this MX Lookup tool to check the mail exchange servers configured for a domain. It is useful for email routing checks, mail setup validation, and DNS troubleshooting.

faq.q: What is an MX record?
faq.a: An MX record tells email systems which mail servers receive messages for a domain.

faq.q: Why would I check MX records?
faq.a: You can use it when setting up email, fixing delivery issues, or confirming mail routing.

name: NS Lookup
intro: Find nameservers for a domain.
description: Use this NS Lookup tool to check which authoritative nameservers are configured for a domain. It is useful for DNS setup, migration checks, and troubleshooting.

faq.q: What does NS Lookup show?
faq.a: It shows the authoritative nameservers assigned to the domain.

faq.q: When should I use it?
faq.a: Use it when checking DNS delegation, moving domains, or troubleshooting DNS propagation issues.

name: TXT Lookup
intro: Find TXT records for a domain.
description: Use this TXT Lookup tool to check TXT records for a domain. It is useful for SPF, DKIM, verification records, and general DNS inspection.

faq.q: What can TXT records contain?
faq.a: TXT records often contain SPF policies, domain verification tokens, DKIM-related values, and other text-based DNS data.

faq.q: Why would I check TXT records?
faq.a: You can use it for email authentication checks, verification setup, and DNS troubleshooting.

name: CNAME Lookup
intro: Look up CNAME DNS records for any domain.
description: Use this CNAME lookup tool to check canonical name records for a domain or subdomain. It is useful for DNS troubleshooting, CDN setup, and domain verification.

faq.q: What does a CNAME record do?
faq.a: A CNAME record points one hostname to another hostname instead of directly to an IP address.

faq.q: Can I check subdomains?
faq.a: Yes. You can enter a full subdomain like www.example.com.

name: SOA Lookup
intro: Look up SOA DNS records for any domain.
description: Use this SOA lookup tool to inspect the Start of Authority record for a domain. It helps with DNS zone checks, nameserver validation, and troubleshooting DNS configuration.

faq.q: What information is in an SOA record?
faq.a: An SOA record usually includes the primary nameserver, responsible email, serial number, refresh, retry, expire, and minimum TTL values.

faq.q: Why check an SOA record?
faq.a: It helps confirm that a DNS zone is configured correctly and can reveal zone serial and timing values.

name: SRV Lookup
intro: Look up SRV DNS records for any service domain.
description: Use this SRV lookup tool to find service records for protocols like SIP, XMPP, LDAP, and other services. It is helpful for troubleshooting service discovery and domain-based routing.

faq.q: What format should I enter?
faq.a: Enter a full SRV hostname like _sip._tcp.example.com.

faq.q: What does an SRV record contain?
faq.a: It usually includes priority, weight, port, and target host.

name: DNS Propagation Checker
intro: Check whether DNS records resolve across public DNS resolvers.
description: Use this DNS propagation checker to test whether a domain resolves across multiple public DNS resolvers. It is useful after DNS changes, nameserver updates, or record migrations.

faq.q: What should I enter?
faq.a: Enter a domain or subdomain like example.com or www.example.com.

faq.q: Does this check every DNS server worldwide?
faq.a: No. It checks a set of public resolvers to give a practical propagation snapshot.

name: Open Graph Checker
intro: Check Open Graph tags on any web page.
description: Use this Open Graph checker to inspect og:title, og:description, og:image, og:url, and other social preview tags on a page. It is useful for SEO, social sharing, and debugging link previews.

faq.q: Which tags are checked?
faq.a: The tool checks common Open Graph tags like og:title, og:description, og:image, og:url, og:type, and og:site_name.

faq.q: Do I need the full URL?
faq.a: Yes. Enter the full page URL, including https://.

name: Website Header Checker
intro: Check HTTP response headers for a website.
description: Use this website header checker to inspect HTTP response headers such as content type, cache control, server, and security-related headers. It is useful for debugging websites, APIs, and SEO issues.

faq.q: What does this tool check?
faq.a: It retrieves HTTP response headers for the target website and shows them in a readable format.

faq.q: Can I use a full URL?
faq.a: Yes. You can enter a full URL like https://example.com.

name: Website Redirect Checker
intro: Check whether a URL redirects and where it ends up.
description: Use this website redirect checker to test redirects for a page URL and see the final resolved destination. It is useful for SEO, canonical checks, migration validation, and debugging HTTP redirects.

faq.q: What can I test with this tool?
faq.a: You can test whether a URL redirects and compare the original URL with the final destination.

faq.q: Why is this useful for SEO?
faq.a: It helps confirm that pages redirect to the correct canonical or migrated URL.

name: SSL Certificate Checker
intro: Inspect SSL certificate details for a domain.
description: Use this SSL certificate checker to view certificate details for a domain, including issuer, subject, validity dates, signature algorithm, and more. It is useful for HTTPS troubleshooting and website audits.

faq.q: What details can I see?
faq.a: The tool can show issuer, subject, common name, validity period, and related certificate information.

faq.q: Do I need to enter https:// ?
faq.a: No. A plain domain like example.com is enough.

name: SSL Expiry Checker
intro: Check SSL certificate expiration for a domain.
description: Use this SSL expiry checker to see when a website certificate expires and how many days remain before expiration. It is useful for monitoring HTTPS certificates and preventing downtime.

faq.q: What does this tool show?
faq.a: It shows the certificate expiration date and the number of days left until expiry.

faq.q: Why does SSL expiry matter?
faq.a: An expired SSL certificate can trigger browser warnings and break trust for visitors.

name: TLS Version Checker
intro: Check supported TLS versions for a domain.
description: Use this TLS version checker to inspect which TLS protocol versions are supported by a website. It is useful for HTTPS audits, compatibility checks, and security reviews.

faq.q: What versions can this tool detect?
faq.a: It can show supported TLS versions such as TLS 1.0, 1.1, 1.2, and 1.3 when available.

faq.q: Why is this useful?
faq.a: It helps identify outdated protocol support and confirm modern TLS compatibility.
